subcategory: large tropical ornamental ginger with pendulous flower clusters

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to consistently moist soil in partial shade after last frost; shell ginger is one of the most spectacular ornamental gingers for subtropical gardens, producing long, drooping racemes of waxy, porcelain-white buds that open to reveal pink-and-yellow-marked flowers in spring and summer. In zone 8 the top growth may die back in hard freezes but the rhizomes survive and resprout vigorously; in zones 9–11 it remains semi-evergreen. Plant divisions 2–3 feet apart. The variegated cultivar 'Variegata' (yellow-striped leaves) is widely grown as an ornamental.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade after last frost; shell ginger is native to East Asia and produces one of the most beautiful inflorescences in the ginger family — long, pendulous, branched racemes of porcelain-white, waxy, egg-shaped buds that dangle like strings of shells before opening to reveal flowers with white petals and a broad, yellow-and-red-marked labellum. The broadly lance-shaped, glossy, deep-green leaves are large and handsome, providing a lush tropical effect throughout the growing season, and the leaves and rhizomes are used in Southeast Asian cooking to wrap foods and flavor dishes. In zones 8–9 the aerial stems may be killed by hard freezes but rhizomes typically survive the winter underground and resprout with vigor in spring; in zones 10–11 it is essentially evergreen. Clumps spread steadily over time and should be divided every 3–5 years to maintain vigor. The popular variegated form 'Variegata' has striking yellow-and-green-striped foliage and is slightly less vigorous but equally ornamental.

subcategory: shade-tolerant edible Japanese ginger grown for flower buds

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full shade to partial shade; mioga ginger is one of the most useful shade-tolerant edibles for woodland gardens and north-facing beds, thriving where most food plants will not grow. Unlike culinary ginger, it is reliably cold-hardy to zone 6. The edible portions are the unopened flower buds that emerge directly from the ground in summer and fall; harvest before they fully open and use fresh, pickled, or as a garnish in Japanese cuisine. The rhizome has a more subtle flavor than Zingiber officinale and is not commonly used.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in shade or deep partial shade; mioga ginger is a Japanese culinary plant of major importance in traditional Japanese cooking and is distinguished from all other commonly grown gingers by its exceptional tolerance of deep shade — it is among the very best shade-tolerant edibles available to gardeners in zones 6–9, thriving under deciduous tree canopy where most vegetables and herbs fail entirely. The edible product is the compact, tightly packed, pale-yellow to pinkish flower buds (called 'myoga') that emerge directly from the ground at the base of the plant in summer and fall, separate from the leafy stems; these buds are harvested just before they open and used in Japanese cuisine as a garnish and flavoring for cold tofu, somen noodles, miso soup, and pickled preparations, with a flavor that is milder, more floral, and more delicate than culinary ginger rhizome. The plant goes fully dormant in winter and is reliably cold-hardy well into zone 6; established clumps expand steadily and can be divided every few years. One of the most underutilized shade-garden edibles in North American horticulture.

subcategory: compact Himalayan ginger relative with yellow and red flowers

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; cautleya is a compact Himalayan ginger relative suited to woodland garden conditions and shaded borders, producing erect spikes of bright yellow flowers with contrasting dark red bracts in mid to late summer. It is considerably more cold-hardy than most ornamental gingers and can be grown as a reliable perennial in zones 6–9. Divide established clumps every 3 years in spring. Excellent companion for hostas, ferns, and other shade-tolerant perennials.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade in spring; cautleya is a lesser-known but highly ornamental Himalayan ginger relative (family Zingiberaceae) native to the mountain forests of Nepal, northern India, and southwestern China, where it grows in cool, moist, shaded conditions at moderate to high elevations — making it significantly more cold-tolerant than most ornamental gingers. The plant forms compact, well-behaved clumps of ginger-family foliage (narrow, lance-shaped, mid-green leaves on erect stems) reaching 18–24 inches, topped in midsummer with erect spikes of bright yellow, two-lipped flowers emerging from striking dark maroon-red bracts; the color combination is vivid and exotic. It is fully dormant in winter and hardy to approximately zone 6 with good drainage. Cautleya spicata is an excellent choice for shaded mixed borders, woodland gardens, or container displays where a ginger-family plant of manageable scale and good hardiness is needed; it pairs exceptionally well with hostas, astilbes, and hardy ferns in the part-shade border.

subcategory: hardy Himalayan ginger relative with orchid-like purple flowers

planting.note: Plant fleshy root divisions 3–4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; roscoea emerges late in spring (do not assume dormant divisions are dead) and blooms in early to midsummer with striking purple, orchid-like flowers. It is one of the hardiest ginger relatives available, tolerating winters well into zone 6. Excellent drainage is essential in winter; heavy, wet soils cause root rot. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring.

directions: Plant fleshy root divisions 3–4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; roscoea is a remarkable member of the ginger family (Zingiberaceae) native to the high-altitude grasslands and open forests of the Himalayas — Nepal, northern India, and Bhutan — that has evolved to tolerate cold far exceeding what most Zingiberaceae endure, making it genuinely hardy into USDA zone 6 with excellent drainage. The plant emerges very late in spring (mid-May to early June in most zones; do not disturb the site or assume divisions have failed), quickly producing short, compact rosettes of upright, strap-shaped, mid-green leaves from which the strikingly orchid-like flowers emerge on short stems in early to midsummer; the flowers are large for the plant's scale, strongly two-lipped, in shades of rich violet-purple to purple-blue, with a distinctive hooded upper petal and broad, spreading lower lip that creates a strong resemblance to an orchid bloom. Excellent drainage — particularly in winter when the plant is dormant — is essential to prevent rot of the fleshy storage roots. An unusual and rewarding plant for the shade or woodland garden in zones 6–9.

subcategory: summer-blooming bulb with bare-stem pink flowers; foliage dies before bloom

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer to early fall (after flowering if buying blooming bulbs, or August–September otherwise) with the neck of the bulb at or just below soil level in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; resurrection lily requires excellent drainage and a dry summer dormancy period — summer irrigation will rot the bulbs. The foliage emerges in late winter or early spring, matures and dies completely by early summer, then bare flower stems arise seemingly from nowhere in August without any foliage — giving the plant its common name. Do not disturb established clumps.

directions: Plant bulbs in late summer to early fall with the neck at or just below soil level in excellently well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade under deciduous trees; lycoris squamigera is one of the most dramatic and conversation-provoking bulbs for temperate and subtropical gardens, operating on a uniquely counterintuitive growth cycle that never fails to surprise: broad, strap-like, daffodil-like leaves emerge in late winter or early spring and grow through spring, then die completely and disappear by early summer, leaving bare ground — and then in August, with no warning and no foliage, stout, bare, leafless 18–24-inch stems rise from the ground and open umbels of 6–8 large, fragrant, soft pink to lavender-pink, trumpet-shaped flowers at the top, giving rise to the evocative common names resurrection lily and magic lily. The critical cultural requirement is dry summer conditions: irrigation during the summer dormancy period will rot the bulbs. Plant under deciduous trees (which provide shade and dry conditions under the canopy in summer) or in borders where summer irrigation can be withheld. Bulbs resent disturbance and may skip flowering for 1–2 years after transplanting; once settled, established clumps bloom reliably for decades.

subcategory: late-summer bulb with brilliant yellow spider-like flowers on bare stems

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer to early fall with the neck of the bulb at or just at soil level in excellently well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; golden spider lily requires dry summer dormancy — do not irrigate during the summer when foliage has died. Less cold-hardy than Lycoris squamigera, it is best suited to zones 7–10 and may need mulch protection in zone 7. Foliage emerges in fall after blooming, persists through winter, then dies in spring before summer dormancy. Avoid disturbing established clumps.

directions: Plant bulbs in late summer to early fall with the neck at or just at soil level in excellently well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; golden spider lily operates on the same striking growth cycle as other Lycoris species — strap-like foliage emerges in fall after flowering, persists through the winter months, then dies completely in spring, leaving bare ground through summer before the naked flower stems emerge in late summer bearing brilliant, vivid golden-yellow flower clusters. The flowers themselves are among the most distinctive in the bulb world: the tepals are strongly reflexed and curled back, and the stamens and style extend far beyond the petals in the long, spidery fashion that gives the plant its common name, creating a radiating, starburst effect in an intensely saturated, warm golden yellow. Less cold-hardy than the pink-flowered L. squamigera and L. radiata, it is best suited to zones 7–10 where winters are mild enough to protect the relatively tender bulbs. Like all lycoris, it requires dry summer conditions during dormancy and resents disturbance; established clumps may skip a bloom cycle after transplanting but then flower reliably for many years.
